Masashige]][[sq:Kusunoki Masashige]][[sr:Кусуноки Масашиге]][[th:คูซูโนกิ มาซาชิเงะ]][[tr:Kusunoki Masashige]][[uk:Кусунокі Масашіґе]][[wuu:楠木正成]][[zh:楠木正成]] {{Short description|14th-century Japanese samurai}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=bio|楠木正成|date=October 2021}} {{family name hatnote|Kusunoki|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Kusunoki Masashige | honorific_suffix = [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] | native_name = 楠木正成 | native_name_lang = ja | image = File:Kusunoki Masashige.jpg | caption = Portrait of Kusunoki Masashige by {{ill|Kanō Sanraku|en|Kanō Sanraku|WD=Q3064625|lt=Kanō Sanraku|1=Kanō Sanraku}}, {{circa|before 1635}} | birth_date = 1294 | birth_place ={{ill|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|1=Kawachi Province}} | death_date = {{death date|1336|7|4}} | death_place ={{ill|Minato River|WD=Q11563141|1=Minato River}} | resting_place = {{ill|Kanshin-ji|WD=Q1088470|1=Kanshin-ji}} | other_names = Dai Nankō, Hyōe-no-Jō, Saemon-no-Jō, Jō | occupation = {{ill|bushi|WD=Q1975935|1=bushi}}; {{ill|samurai|WD=Q38142|1=Samurai}} | years_active = | known_for = Overthrowing the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, ideal samurai loyalty | notable_works = | monuments = Hōken-tō
Various statues | gender= {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} | nationality= {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17|1=Japan}} | title= {{ill|Kebiishi|WD=Q497217|1=Office of Police and Justice}}; {{ill|Saemonnojō|WD=Q11479716|1=Saemonnojō}}; {{ill|bugyō|WD=Q2535820|1=Bugyō}}; {{ill|shugo|WD=Q1141023|1=Shugo}} | children= {{ill|Kusunoki Masanori|WD=Q11541193|1=Kusunoki Masanori}}; {{ill|Kusunoki Masatoki|WD=Q11541199|1=Kusunoki Masatoki}}; {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|WD=Q966298|1=Kusunoki Masatsura}} | father= {{ill|Kusunoki Masatoyo|WD=Q11541204|1=Kusunoki Masatoyo}} | relatives= {{ill|Kusunoki Masasue|WD=Q11541197|1=Kusunoki Masasue}} | family= {{ill|Kusunoki clan|WD=Q11541208|1=Kusunoki clan}} | organization= {{ill|Q11507117|WD=Q11507117|1=Q11507117}}; {{ill|Sanboku Issō|WD=Q11355573|1=Sanboku Issō}} | given_name= {{ill|Masashige|WD=Q6782681|1=Masashige}} }} [[Image:Kusunoki Masashige.JPG|right|thumb|300px|{{ill|Equestrian statue|en|Equestrian statue|WD=Q659396|lt=Equestrian statue|1=Equestrian statue}} of Kusunoki Masashige outside the [[:en:Imperial Palace of Japan|Imperial Palace]] in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}.]] [[Image:Kusunoki Masashige statue.jpg|right|thumb|The same statue from a different angle, close-up.]] {{Nihongo|'''Kusunoki Masashige'''|楠木 正成||1294 – 4 July 1336}} was a Japanese military commander and {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} of the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} remembered as the ideal loyal samurai. Kusunoki fought for {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}} in the {{ill|Genkō War|en|Genkō War|WD=Q1432624|lt=Genkō War|1=Genkō War}} to overthrow the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}} and restore power in Japan to the {{ill|Imperial Court in Kyoto|en|Imperial Court in Kyoto|WD=Q11680900|lt=Imperial Court|1=Imperial Court in Kyoto}}. Kusunoki was a leading figure of the [[Kenmu Restoration]] in 1333, and remained loyal to the unpopular Emperor Go-Daigo after {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} began to reverse the restoration in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō wars|1=Nanboku-chō period}} three years later. Kusunoki attacked Takauji in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu|1=Settsu Province}} at the command of the Emperor, an act of obedience sure to result in defeat, and died at the {{ill|Battle of Minatogawa|en|Battle of Minatogawa|WD=Q114620|lt=Battle of Minatogawa|1=Battle of Minatogawa}} in 1336. Kusunoki became a popular {{ill|en:Legend|en|en:Legend|WD=Q44342|lt=en:Legend|1=Legend}} in Japan representing loyalty and {{ill|Virtue|en|Virtue|WD=Q157811|lt=Virtue|1=Virtue}}, and associated with the phrase "Would that I had seven lives to give for my country!" (七生報國; "''Shichishō Hōkoku!''"). Kusunoki was posthumously awarded the highest court Rank in Japan, [[Senior First Rank]] (''shō ichi-i)'', by the {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|WD=Q1322181|lt=Government of Meiji Japan|1=Government of Meiji Japan}} in 1880, over 500 years after his death. He was highly evaluated as "Japan's three loyal retainers" along with Fujifusa Madenokoji and {{ill|Taira no Shigemori|en|Taira no Shigemori|WD=Q1184985|lt=Taira no Shigemori|1=Taira no Shigemori}}. ==Early life== Kusunoki Masashige's origin has not been validated and it was merely six years between the start of his military campaign in 1331 and his demise in 1336. Kusunoki is believed to have been born in 1294 in {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}} as a "well-to-do member of the rural {{ill|Gentry|en|Gentry|WD=Q844586|lt=Gentry|1=Gentry}}" and claimed descent from {{ill|Tachibana no Moroe|en|Tachibana no Moroe|WD=Q3135863|lt=Tachibana Moroe|1=Tachibana no Moroe}}, "a great nobleman" of the eighth century. His birthplace has been linked to the village of Chihaya-Akasaka where a small monument called the "Nanko Tanjochi" can be found. Kusunoki was a "scholar and a devout {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism in Japan}}" with much of his early education taking place at {{ill|Kanshin-ji|en|Kanshin-ji|WD=Q1088470|lt=Kanshin-ji|1=Kanshin-ji}} Temple in Kawachinagano, in present-day southern {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}.{{rp|53}} Later in his life, Kusunoki would arrange for considerable renovations to the temple. While studying at Kanshin-ji, he would make regular trips to central Kawachinagano to study strategy under the tutelage of a man named Oe Tokichika. According to legend, Emperor Go-Daigo had a dream in which he was sheltering under a [[:en:Camphor tree|Camphor tree]] ("''kusunoki''" in {{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|WD=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}}), and that this dream led him to the surname of the warrior who would support him.{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai, A Military History |publisher=MacMillan Publishing Co., Inc. |year=1977 |ISBN=0026205408 |page=95}}{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=158–159}} ==Military career== A brilliant tactician and strategist, Kusunoki's cunning defense of two key Loyalist fortresses at [[:en:Chihayaakasaka, Osaka|Akasaka]], the {{ill|Siege of Akasaka|en|Siege of Akasaka|WD=Q7509791|lt=Siege of Akasaka|1=Siege of Akasaka}}, and [[:en:Chihayaakasaka, Osaka|Chihaya]], the {{ill|Siege of Chihaya|en|Siege of Chihaya|WD=Q7509930|lt=Siege of Chihaya|1=Siege of Chihaya}}, helped allow Go-Daigo to briefly return to power.{{rp|160,164,173,175,180}} He lived during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}. In 1333, Go-Daigo rewarded Masashige with governorship of {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province|1=Izumi Province}} and {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}}. Furthermore, he was promoted to Fifth Rank. Later he was appointed to the Records Office and Settlements Board.{{Cite book |last=Morris |first=Ivan |title=The Nobility of Failure |publisher=Holt, Rinehart and Winston |year=1975 |ISBN=9780030108112 |pages=[https://archive.org/details/nobilityoffailur00morr/page/127 127] |url=https://archive.org/details/nobilityoffailur00morr/page/127 }} However, one of the loyalist generals, {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, betrayed Go-Daigo and led an army against Kusunoki and the remaining loyalists.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334–1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |pages=43–44}} Takauji was able to take Kyoto, but only temporarily before [[Nitta Yoshisada]] and Masashige were able to dislodge Takauji, forcing him to flee to the west. By 1336 however, Takauji was a threat to Kyoto again.{{rp|130}} Kusunoki suggested to the Emperor that they take refuge on sacred {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|WD=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}} and allow Takauji to take {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, only to swoop down from the mountain, and with the help of the monks of Mount Hiei, trap Takauji in the city and destroy him.{{rp|181–182}} Go-Daigo was unwilling to leave the capital however, and insisted that Kusunoki meet Takauji's superior forces in the field in a pitched battle. Kusunoki, in what would later be viewed as the ultimate act of samurai loyalty, obediently accepted his Emperor's foolish command and knowingly marched his army into almost certain death.{{rp|102-102}}{{rp|126}} The battle, which took place at {{ill|Battle of Minatogawa|en|Battle of Minatogawa|WD=Q114620|lt=Minatogawa|1=Battle of Minatogawa}} in modern-day {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kobe|en|Chūō-ku, Kobe|WD=Q686802|lt=Chūō-ku|1=Chūō-ku, Kobe}}, [[:en:Kobe, Hyōgo|Kobe]], was a tactical disaster. There are two accounts of the proposal made by Kusunoki Masashige to the emperor Go-Daigo, the [[Taiheiki]] and the ''Baisho Ron''. One was that they regroup and attack from two sides, the other was that they bring back general Takauji to their side thus balancing the scales. Both arguments were ignored.{{rp|181–183}}{{rp|50–52}} Kusunoki, his army completely surrounded, was down to only 50 of the original 700 horsemen. According to legend, his brother {{ill|Kusunoki Masasue|en|Kusunoki Masasue|WD=Q11541197|lt=Masasue|1=Kusunoki Masasue}}'s last words were ''Shichishō Hōkoku!'' (七生報國; "Would that I had seven lives to give for my country!") and Kusunoki Masashige agreed.{{rp|185–187}}{{rp|133}} Upon his death, his head was removed and sent to {{ill|Kanshin-ji|en|Kanshin-ji|WD=Q1088470|lt=Kanshin-ji|1=Kanshin-ji}} where it was buried in a {{ill|Kubizuka|en|Kubizuka|WD=Q11667022|lt=Kubizuka|1=Kubizuka}}. [[File:Kusunoki Kanshinji.jpg|thumb|Equestrian statue of Kusunoki Masashige at the entrance to {{ill|Kanshin-ji|en|Kanshin-ji|WD=Q1088470|lt=Kanshin-ji|1=Kanshin-ji}} in {{ill|Kawachinagano|en|Kawachinagano|WD=Q819572|lt=Kawachinagano|1=Kawachinagano}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}.]] He is also thought to have built a number of smaller castles throughout southern Osaka, particularly within what is now the city of {{ill|Kawachinagano|en|Kawachinagano|WD=Q819572|lt=Kawachinagano|1=Kawachinagano}}. Eboshigata Castle and Ishibotoke Castle were both built along the route of the Koya Kaido, a popular pilgrimage trail stretching between Kyoto and [[:en:Koyasan|Koyasan]]. These castles were designed not only to protect the trail from bandits but also as an important source of income and intelligence as travelers were obliged to pay a toll and the garrison would listen out for rumours and news from around Japan. ==Legacy== [[File:Tachi Sword - Kagemitsu.jpg|thumb| Kagemitsu Sword of Kusunoki Masashige, also called Little Dragon Kagemitsu (Koryū Kagemitsu) after a relief on the face of the blade, curvature: 2.7 cm (1.1 in) Kamakura period, May 1322 80.6 cm (31.7 in) Tokyo National Museum, Tokyo]] His son, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|Kusunoki Masatsura|WD=Q966298|lt=Kusunoki Masatsura|1=Kusunoki Masatsura}}, served the emperor's successor, the 12-year-old [[:en:Go-Murakami|Go-Murakami]], in a relationship of reciprocal trust and devotion mirroring the figure of his father Kusunoki and keeping the flame of loyalist resistance alive. Masatsura died alongside his brother Masatoki and cousin Wada Takahide in a battle that saw the end of the Kusunoki clan and there followed a less-than-ideal scramble for power and gain among the Courts.{{rp|103}} Kusunoki "stands in the history of his country as the ideal figure of a warrior, compact of civil and military virtues in a high degree."{{rp|53}} The parting of Masashige with his son "used to be included in all elementary school readers and was the subject of a patriotic song which was popular in Japanese schools before {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}."{{rp|131}} Masashige had a [[tachi]] called Little Dragon Kagemitsu (小龍景光, Koryū Kagemitsu). An elaborate Kurikara dragon was carved on the handle. Originally, the dragon's appearance was visible on the blade, but later, in the process of cutting off the handle and shortening the length, the dragon's body was hidden by the handle. The dragon is a manifestation of [[Acala]]. ==Legend== After the full-scale introduction of {{ill|Neo-Confucianism|en|Neo-Confucianism|WD=Q825754|lt=Neo-Confucianism|1=Neo-Confucianism}} as a state philosophy by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], Kusunoki Masashige, once called a traitor by the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court|1=Northern Court}}, was resurrected with {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}} as a precursor of Sinocentric absolutists, based upon the Neo-Confucian theories.{{Citation needed|date=November 2021}} During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, scholars and samurai who were influenced by the Neo-Confucian theories popularized the legend of Kusunoki and enshrined him as a patriotic hero, called '''Nankō''' (楠公) or '''Dai Nankō''' (大楠公), who epitomized {{ill|Loyalty|en|Loyalty|WD=Q1132131|lt=Loyalty|1=Loyalty}}, courage, and {{ill|Faith|en|Faith|WD=Q5410500|lt=devotion|1=Faith}} to the Emperor.{{Citation needed|date=November 2021}} In 1871 [[Minatogawa Shrine]] is established in order to enshrine the [[kami]] spirit of Kusunoki Masashige.{{Citation needed|date=November 2021}} Kusunoki later became a patron saint of sorts to {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} {{ill|Kamikaze|en|Kamikaze|WD=Q133194|lt=Kamikaze|1=Kamikaze}}, who saw themselves as his spiritual heirs in sacrificing their lives for the Emperor.{{Citation needed|date=November 2021}} == Family == Brother: * {{ill|Kusunoki Masasue|en|Kusunoki Masasue|WD=Q11541197|lt=Kusunoki Masasue|1=Kusunoki Masasue}} Children: * {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|Kusunoki Masatsura|WD=Q966298|lt=Kusunoki Masatsura|1=Kusunoki Masatsura}} * {{ill|Kusunoki Masanori|en|Kusunoki Masanori|WD=Q11541193|lt=Kusunoki Masanori|1=Kusunoki Masanori}} * {{ill|Kusunoki Masatoki|en|Kusunoki Masatoki|WD=Q11541199|lt=Kusunoki Masatoki|1=Kusunoki Masatoki}} ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (July 20, 1880; posthumous) ==See also== * {{ill|Nathan Hale|en|Nathan Hale|WD=Q465574|lt=Nathan Hale|1=Nathan Hale}}— {{ill|Americans|en|Americans|WD=Q846570|lt=American|1=Americans}} {{ill|Patriot (American Revolution)|en|Patriot (American Revolution)|WD=Q1365374|lt=Patriot|1=Patriot (American Revolution)}}, soldier, and spy for the {{ill|Continental Army|en|Continental Army|WD=Q54122|lt=Continental Army|1=Continental Army}} during the {{ill|American Revolutionary War|en|American Revolutionary War|WD=Q40949|lt=American Revolutionary War|1=American Revolutionary War}}. He volunteered for an intelligence-gathering mission in {{ill|New York City|en|New York City|WD=Q60|lt=New York City|1=New York City}} but was captured by the {{ill|British people|en|British people|WD=Q842438|lt=British|1=British people}} and executed by {{ill|hanging|en|hanging|WD=Q175111|lt=hanging|1=Hanging}}. His last words were, reportedly, "I only regret that I have but one life to lose for my country." If he was intending to paraphrase or quote anyone, however, it was likely {{ill|Joseph Addison|en|Joseph Addison|WD=Q206384|lt=Joseph Addison|1=Joseph Addison}}'s play {{ill|Cato, a Tragedy|en|Cato, a Tragedy|WD=Q5053587|lt=Cato, a Tragedy|1=Cato, a Tragedy}} and not Kusunoki Masashige. * {{ill|Otoya Yamaguchi|en|Otoya Yamaguchi|WD=Q469406|lt=Otoya Yamaguchi|1=Otoya Yamaguchi}}— 17-year-old Japanese right-wing {{ill|Ultranationalism|en|Ultranationalism|WD=Q38352698|lt=ultranationalist|1=Ultranationalism}} who assassinated socialist politician [[:en:Inejiro Asanuma|Inejiro Asanuma]] during a televised debate in 1960. After being arrested and interrogated, Yamaguchi committed suicide in a detention facility less than three weeks after the assassination. Before committing suicide, Yamaguchi wrote with toothpaste on his cell wall, "Long live the {{ill|Emperor|en|Emperor|WD=Q39018|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor}}" (天皇陛下万才, ''tennōheika banzai'') and "Would that I had seven lives to give for my country" (七生報国, ''shichishō hōkoku'')). ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Kusunoki Masashige}} [[en:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[ca:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[cs:Masašige Kusunoki]] [[da:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[de:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[eo:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[es:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[fa:کوسونوکی ماساشیگه]] [[fi:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[fr:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[id:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[it:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[ja:楠木正成]] [[ka:კუსუნოკი მასასიგე]] [[ko:구스노키 마사시게]] [[nl:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[pl:Masashige Kusunoki]] [[pt:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[ru:Кусуноки Масасигэ]] [[simple:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[sq:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[sr:Кусуноки Масашиге]] [[th:คูซูโนกิ มาซาชิเงะ]] [[tr:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[uk:Кусунокі Масашіґе]] [[wuu:楠木正成]] [[zh:楠木正成]] {{wikidata link|Q382234}} [[Category:1336 deaths]] [[Category:Deified Japanese men]] [[Category:Genkō War]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:People of Kamakura-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Nanboku-chō-period Japan]] [[Category:Samurai]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]]

samurai}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=bio|楠木正成|date=October 2021}} {{family name hatnote|Kusunoki|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Kusunoki Masashige | honorific_suffix = [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] | native_name = 楠木正成 | native_name_lang = ja | image = File:Kusunoki Masashige.jpg | caption = Portrait of Kusunoki Masashige by {{ill|Kanō Sanraku|en|Kanō Sanraku|WD=Q3064625|lt=Kanō Sanraku|1=Kanō Sanraku}}, {{circa|before 1635}} | birth_date = 1294 | birth_place ={{ill|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|1=Kawachi Province}} | death_date = {{death date|1336|7|4}} | death_place ={{ill|Minato River|WD=Q11563141|1=Minato River}} | resting_place = {{ill|Kanshin-ji|WD=Q1088470|1=Kanshin-ji}} | other_names = Dai Nankō, Hyōe-no-Jō, Saemon-no-Jō, Jō | occupation = {{ill|bushi|WD=Q1975935|1=bushi}}; {{ill|samurai|WD=Q38142|1=Samurai}} | years_active = | known_for = Overthrowing the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, ideal samurai loyalty | notable_works = | monuments = Hōken-tō
Various statues | gender= {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} | nationality= {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17|1=Japan}} | title= {{ill|Kebiishi|WD=Q497217|1=Office of Police and Justice}}; {{ill|Saemonnojō|WD=Q11479716|1=Saemonnojō}}; {{ill|bugyō|WD=Q2535820|1=Bugyō}}; {{ill|shugo|WD=Q1141023|1=Shugo}} | children= {{ill|Kusunoki Masanori|WD=Q11541193|1=Kusunoki Masanori}}; {{ill|Kusunoki Masatoki|WD=Q11541199|1=Kusunoki Masatoki}}; {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|WD=Q966298|1=Kusunoki Masatsura}} | father= {{ill|Kusunoki Masatoyo|WD=Q11541204|1=Kusunoki Masatoyo}} | relatives= {{ill|Kusunoki Masasue|WD=Q11541197|1=Kusunoki Masasue}} | family= {{ill|Kusunoki clan|WD=Q11541208|1=Kusunoki clan}} | organization= {{ill|Q11507117|WD=Q11507117|1=Q11507117}}; {{ill|Sanboku Issō|WD=Q11355573|1=Sanboku Issō}} | given_name= {{ill|Masashige|WD=Q6782681|1=Masashige}} }} [[Image:Kusunoki Masashige.JPG|right|thumb|300px|{{ill|Equestrian statue|en|Equestrian statue|WD=Q659396|lt=Equestrian statue|1=Equestrian statue}} of Kusunoki Masashige outside the [[:en:Imperial Palace of Japan|Imperial Palace]] in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}.]] [[Image:Kusunoki Masashige statue.jpg|right|thumb|The same statue from a different angle, close-up.]] {{Nihongo|'''Kusunoki Masashige'''|楠木 正成||1294 – 4 July 1336}} was a Japanese military commander and {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} of the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} remembered as the ideal loyal samurai. Kusunoki fought for {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}} in the {{ill|Genkō War|en|Genkō War|WD=Q1432624|lt=Genkō War|1=Genkō War}} to overthrow the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}} and restore power in Japan to the {{ill|Imperial Court in Kyoto|en|Imperial Court in Kyoto|WD=Q11680900|lt=Imperial Court|1=Imperial Court in Kyoto}}. Kusunoki was a leading figure of the [[Kenmu Restoration]] in 1333, and remained loyal to the unpopular Emperor Go-Daigo after {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} began to reverse the restoration in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō wars|1=Nanboku-chō period}} three years later. Kusunoki attacked Takauji in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu|1=Settsu Province}} at the command of the Emperor, an act of obedience sure to result in defeat, and died at the {{ill|Battle of Minatogawa|en|Battle of Minatogawa|WD=Q114620|lt=Battle of Minatogawa|1=Battle of Minatogawa}} in 1336. Kusunoki became a popular {{ill|en:Legend|en|en:Legend|WD=Q44342|lt=en:Legend|1=Legend}} in Japan representing loyalty and {{ill|Virtue|en|Virtue|WD=Q157811|lt=Virtue|1=Virtue}}, and associated with the phrase "Would that I had seven lives to give for my country!" (七生報國; "''Shichishō Hōkoku!''"). Kusunoki was posthumously awarded the highest court Rank in Japan, [[Senior First Rank]] (''shō ichi-i)'', by the {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|WD=Q1322181|lt=Government of Meiji Japan|1=Government of Meiji Japan}} in 1880, over 500 years after his death. He was highly evaluated as "Japan's three loyal retainers" along with Fujifusa Madenokoji and {{ill|Taira no Shigemori|en|Taira no Shigemori|WD=Q1184985|lt=Taira no Shigemori|1=Taira no Shigemori}}. ==Early life== Kusunoki Masashige's origin has not been validated and it was merely six years between the start of his military campaign in 1331 and his demise in 1336. Kusunoki is believed to have been born in 1294 in {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}} as a "well-to-do member of the rural {{ill|Gentry|en|Gentry|WD=Q844586|lt=Gentry|1=Gentry}}" and claimed descent from {{ill|Tachibana no Moroe|en|Tachibana no Moroe|WD=Q3135863|lt=Tachibana Moroe|1=Tachibana no Moroe}}, "a great nobleman" of the eighth century. His birthplace has been linked to the village of Chihaya-Akasaka where a small monument called the "Nanko Tanjochi" can be found. Kusunoki was a "scholar and a devout {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism in Japan}}" with much of his early education taking place at {{ill|Kanshin-ji|en|Kanshin-ji|WD=Q1088470|lt=Kanshin-ji|1=Kanshin-ji}} Temple in Kawachinagano, in present-day southern {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}.{{rp|53}} Later in his life, Kusunoki would arrange for considerable renovations to the temple. While studying at Kanshin-ji, he would make regular trips to central Kawachinagano to study strategy under the tutelage of a man named Oe Tokichika. According to legend, Emperor Go-Daigo had a dream in which he was sheltering under a [[:en:Camphor tree|Camphor tree]] ("''kusunoki''" in {{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|WD=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}}), and that this dream led him to the surname of the warrior who would support him.{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai, A Military History |publisher=MacMillan Publishing Co., Inc. |year=1977 |ISBN=0026205408 |page=95}}{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=158–159}} ==Military career== A brilliant tactician and strategist, Kusunoki's cunning defense of two key Loyalist fortresses at [[:en:Chihayaakasaka, Osaka|Akasaka]], the {{ill|Siege of Akasaka|en|Siege of Akasaka|WD=Q7509791|lt=Siege of Akasaka|1=Siege of Akasaka}}, and [[:en:Chihayaakasaka, Osaka|Chihaya]], the {{ill|Siege of Chihaya|en|Siege of Chihaya|WD=Q7509930|lt=Siege of Chihaya|1=Siege of Chihaya}}, helped allow Go-Daigo to briefly return to power.{{rp|160,164,173,175,180}} He lived during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}. In 1333, Go-Daigo rewarded Masashige with governorship of {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province|1=Izumi Province}} and {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}}. Furthermore, he was promoted to Fifth Rank. Later he was appointed to the Records Office and Settlements Board.{{Cite book |last=Morris |first=Ivan |title=The Nobility of Failure |publisher=Holt, Rinehart and Winston |year=1975 |ISBN=9780030108112 |pages=[https://archive.org/details/nobilityoffailur00morr/page/127 127] |url=https://archive.org/details/nobilityoffailur00morr/page/127 }} However, one of the loyalist generals, {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, betrayed Go-Daigo and led an army against Kusunoki and the remaining loyalists.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334–1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |pages=43–44}} Takauji was able to take Kyoto, but only temporarily before [[Nitta Yoshisada]] and Masashige were able to dislodge Takauji, forcing him to flee to the west. By 1336 however, Takauji was a threat to Kyoto again.{{rp|130}} Kusunoki suggested to the Emperor that they take refuge on sacred {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|WD=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}} and allow Takauji to take {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, only to swoop down from the mountain, and with the help of the monks of Mount Hiei, trap Takauji in the city and destroy him.{{rp|181–182}} Go-Daigo was unwilling to leave the capital however, and insisted that Kusunoki meet Takauji's superior forces in the field in a pitched battle. Kusunoki, in what would later be viewed as the ultimate act of samurai loyalty, obediently accepted his Emperor's foolish command and knowingly marched his army into almost certain death.{{rp|102-102}}{{rp|126}} The battle, which took place at {{ill|Battle of Minatogawa|en|Battle of Minatogawa|WD=Q114620|lt=Minatogawa|1=Battle of Minatogawa}} in modern-day {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kobe|en|Chūō-ku, Kobe|WD=Q686802|lt=Chūō-ku|1=Chūō-ku, Kobe}}, [[:en:Kobe, Hyōgo|Kobe]], was a tactical disaster. There are two accounts of the proposal made by Kusunoki Masashige to the emperor Go-Daigo, the [[Taiheiki]] and the ''Baisho Ron''. One was that they regroup and attack from two sides, the other was that they bring back general Takauji to their side thus balancing the scales. Both arguments were ignored.{{rp|181–183}}{{rp|50–52}} Kusunoki, his army completely surrounded, was down to only 50 of the original 700 horsemen. According to legend, his brother {{ill|Kusunoki Masasue|en|Kusunoki Masasue|WD=Q11541197|lt=Masasue|1=Kusunoki Masasue}}'s last words were ''Shichishō Hōkoku!'' (七生報國; "Would that I had seven lives to give for my country!") and Kusunoki Masashige agreed.{{rp|185–187}}{{rp|133}} Upon his death, his head was removed and sent to {{ill|Kanshin-ji|en|Kanshin-ji|WD=Q1088470|lt=Kanshin-ji|1=Kanshin-ji}} where it was buried in a {{ill|Kubizuka|en|Kubizuka|WD=Q11667022|lt=Kubizuka|1=Kubizuka}}. [[File:Kusunoki Kanshinji.jpg|thumb|Equestrian statue of Kusunoki Masashige at the entrance to {{ill|Kanshin-ji|en|Kanshin-ji|WD=Q1088470|lt=Kanshin-ji|1=Kanshin-ji}} in {{ill|Kawachinagano|en|Kawachinagano|WD=Q819572|lt=Kawachinagano|1=Kawachinagano}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}.]] He is also thought to have built a number of smaller castles throughout southern Osaka, particularly within what is now the city of {{ill|Kawachinagano|en|Kawachinagano|WD=Q819572|lt=Kawachinagano|1=Kawachinagano}}. Eboshigata Castle and Ishibotoke Castle were both built along the route of the Koya Kaido, a popular pilgrimage trail stretching between Kyoto and [[:en:Koyasan|Koyasan]]. These castles were designed not only to protect the trail from bandits but also as an important source of income and intelligence as travelers were obliged to pay a toll and the garrison would listen out for rumours and news from around Japan. ==Legacy== [[File:Tachi Sword - Kagemitsu.jpg|thumb| Kagemitsu Sword of Kusunoki Masashige, also called Little Dragon Kagemitsu (Koryū Kagemitsu) after a relief on the face of the blade, curvature: 2.7 cm (1.1 in) Kamakura period, May 1322 80.6 cm (31.7 in) Tokyo National Museum, Tokyo]] His son, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|Kusunoki Masatsura|WD=Q966298|lt=Kusunoki Masatsura|1=Kusunoki Masatsura}}, served the emperor's successor, the 12-year-old [[:en:Go-Murakami|Go-Murakami]], in a relationship of reciprocal trust and devotion mirroring the figure of his father Kusunoki and keeping the flame of loyalist resistance alive. Masatsura died alongside his brother Masatoki and cousin Wada Takahide in a battle that saw the end of the Kusunoki clan and there followed a less-than-ideal scramble for power and gain among the Courts.{{rp|103}} Kusunoki "stands in the history of his country as the ideal figure of a warrior, compact of civil and military virtues in a high degree."{{rp|53}} The parting of Masashige with his son "used to be included in all elementary school readers and was the subject of a patriotic song which was popular in Japanese schools before {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}."{{rp|131}} Masashige had a [[tachi]] called Little Dragon Kagemitsu (小龍景光, Koryū Kagemitsu). An elaborate Kurikara dragon was carved on the handle. Originally, the dragon's appearance was visible on the blade, but later, in the process of cutting off the handle and shortening the length, the dragon's body was hidden by the handle. The dragon is a manifestation of [[Acala]]. ==Legend== After the full-scale introduction of {{ill|Neo-Confucianism|en|Neo-Confucianism|WD=Q825754|lt=Neo-Confucianism|1=Neo-Confucianism}} as a state philosophy by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], Kusunoki Masashige, once called a traitor by the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court|1=Northern Court}}, was resurrected with {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}} as a precursor of Sinocentric absolutists, based upon the Neo-Confucian theories.{{Citation needed|date=November 2021}} During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, scholars and samurai who were influenced by the Neo-Confucian theories popularized the legend of Kusunoki and enshrined him as a patriotic hero, called '''Nankō''' (楠公) or '''Dai Nankō''' (大楠公), who epitomized {{ill|Loyalty|en|Loyalty|WD=Q1132131|lt=Loyalty|1=Loyalty}}, courage, and {{ill|Faith|en|Faith|WD=Q5410500|lt=devotion|1=Faith}} to the Emperor.{{Citation needed|date=November 2021}} In 1871 [[Minatogawa Shrine]] is established in order to enshrine the [[kami]] spirit of Kusunoki Masashige.{{Citation needed|date=November 2021}} Kusunoki later became a patron saint of sorts to {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} {{ill|Kamikaze|en|Kamikaze|WD=Q133194|lt=Kamikaze|1=Kamikaze}}, who saw themselves as his spiritual heirs in sacrificing their lives for the Emperor.{{Citation needed|date=November 2021}} == Family == Brother: * {{ill|Kusunoki Masasue|en|Kusunoki Masasue|WD=Q11541197|lt=Kusunoki Masasue|1=Kusunoki Masasue}} Children: * {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|Kusunoki Masatsura|WD=Q966298|lt=Kusunoki Masatsura|1=Kusunoki Masatsura}} * {{ill|Kusunoki Masanori|en|Kusunoki Masanori|WD=Q11541193|lt=Kusunoki Masanori|1=Kusunoki Masanori}} * {{ill|Kusunoki Masatoki|en|Kusunoki Masatoki|WD=Q11541199|lt=Kusunoki Masatoki|1=Kusunoki Masatoki}} ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (July 20, 1880; posthumous) ==See also== * {{ill|Nathan Hale|en|Nathan Hale|WD=Q465574|lt=Nathan Hale|1=Nathan Hale}}— {{ill|Americans|en|Americans|WD=Q846570|lt=American|1=Americans}} {{ill|Patriot (American Revolution)|en|Patriot (American Revolution)|WD=Q1365374|lt=Patriot|1=Patriot (American Revolution)}}, soldier, and spy for the {{ill|Continental Army|en|Continental Army|WD=Q54122|lt=Continental Army|1=Continental Army}} during the {{ill|American Revolutionary War|en|American Revolutionary War|WD=Q40949|lt=American Revolutionary War|1=American Revolutionary War}}. He volunteered for an intelligence-gathering mission in {{ill|New York City|en|New York City|WD=Q60|lt=New York City|1=New York City}} but was captured by the {{ill|British people|en|British people|WD=Q842438|lt=British|1=British people}} and executed by {{ill|hanging|en|hanging|WD=Q175111|lt=hanging|1=Hanging}}. His last words were, reportedly, "I only regret that I have but one life to lose for my country." If he was intending to paraphrase or quote anyone, however, it was likely {{ill|Joseph Addison|en|Joseph Addison|WD=Q206384|lt=Joseph Addison|1=Joseph Addison}}'s play {{ill|Cato, a Tragedy|en|Cato, a Tragedy|WD=Q5053587|lt=Cato, a Tragedy|1=Cato, a Tragedy}} and not Kusunoki Masashige. * {{ill|Otoya Yamaguchi|en|Otoya Yamaguchi|WD=Q469406|lt=Otoya Yamaguchi|1=Otoya Yamaguchi}}— 17-year-old Japanese right-wing {{ill|Ultranationalism|en|Ultranationalism|WD=Q38352698|lt=ultranationalist|1=Ultranationalism}} who assassinated socialist politician [[:en:Inejiro Asanuma|Inejiro Asanuma]] during a televised debate in 1960. After being arrested and interrogated, Yamaguchi committed suicide in a detention facility less than three weeks after the assassination. Before committing suicide, Yamaguchi wrote with toothpaste on his cell wall, "Long live the {{ill|Emperor|en|Emperor|WD=Q39018|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor}}" (天皇陛下万才, ''tennōheika banzai'') and "Would that I had seven lives to give for my country" (七生報国, ''shichishō hōkoku'')). ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Kusunoki Masashige}} [[en:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[ca:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[cs:Masašige Kusunoki]] [[da:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[de:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[eo:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[es:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[fa:کوسونوکی ماساشیگه]] [[fi:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[fr:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[id:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[it:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[ja:楠木正成]] [[ka:კუსუნოკი მასასიგე]] [[ko:구스노키 마사시게]] [[nl:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[pl:Masashige Kusunoki]] [[pt:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[ru:Кусуноки Масасигэ]] [[simple:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[sq:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[sr:Кусуноки Масашиге]] [[th:คูซูโนกิ มาซาชิเงะ]] [[tr:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[uk:Кусунокі Масашіґе]] [[wuu:楠木正成]] [[zh:楠木正成]] {{wikidata link|Q382234}} [[Category:1336 deaths]] [[Category:Deified Japanese men]] [[Category:Genkō War]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:People of Kamakura-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Nanboku-chō-period Japan]] [[Category:Samurai]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

samurai}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=bio|楠木正成|date=October 2021}} {{family name hatnote|Kusunoki|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Kusunoki Masashige | honorific_suffix = [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] | native_name = 楠木正成 | native_name_lang = ja | image = File:Kusunoki Masashige.jpg | caption = Portrait of Kusunoki Masashige by {{ill|Kanō Sanraku|en|Kanō Sanraku|WD=Q3064625|lt=Kanō Sanraku|1=Kanō Sanraku}}, {{circa|before 1635}} | birth_date = 1294 | birth_place ={{ill|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|1=Kawachi Province}} | death_date = {{death date|1336|7|4}} | death_place ={{ill|Minato River|WD=Q11563141|1=Minato River}} | resting_place = {{ill|Kanshin-ji|WD=Q1088470|1=Kanshin-ji}} | other_names = Dai Nankō, Hyōe-no-Jō, Saemon-no-Jō, Jō | occupation = {{ill|bushi|WD=Q1975935|1=bushi}}; {{ill|samurai|WD=Q38142|1=Samurai}} | years_active = | known_for = Overthrowing the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, ideal samurai loyalty | notable_works = | monuments = Hōken-tō
Various statues | gender= {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} | nationality= {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17|1=Japan}} | title= {{ill|Kebiishi|WD=Q497217|1=Office of Police and Justice}}; {{ill|Saemonnojō|WD=Q11479716|1=Saemonnojō}}; {{ill|bugyō|WD=Q2535820|1=Bugyō}}; {{ill|shugo|WD=Q1141023|1=Shugo}} | children= {{ill|Kusunoki Masanori|WD=Q11541193|1=Kusunoki Masanori}}; {{ill|Kusunoki Masatoki|WD=Q11541199|1=Kusunoki Masatoki}}; {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|WD=Q966298|1=Kusunoki Masatsura}} | father= {{ill|Kusunoki Masatoyo|WD=Q11541204|1=Kusunoki Masatoyo}} | relatives= {{ill|Kusunoki Masasue|WD=Q11541197|1=Kusunoki Masasue}} | family= {{ill|Kusunoki clan|WD=Q11541208|1=Kusunoki clan}} | organization= {{ill|Q11507117|WD=Q11507117|1=Q11507117}}; {{ill|Sanboku Issō|WD=Q11355573|1=Sanboku Issō}} | given_name= {{ill|Masashige|WD=Q6782681|1=Masashige}} }} [[Image:Kusunoki Masashige.JPG|right|thumb|300px|{{ill|Equestrian statue|en|Equestrian statue|WD=Q659396|lt=Equestrian statue|1=Equestrian statue}} of Kusunoki Masashige outside the [[:en:Imperial Palace of Japan|Imperial Palace]] in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}.]] [[Image:Kusunoki Masashige statue.jpg|right|thumb|The same statue from a different angle, close-up.]] {{Nihongo|'''Kusunoki Masashige'''|楠木 正成||1294 – 4 July 1336}} was a Japanese military commander and {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} of the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} remembered as the ideal loyal samurai. Kusunoki fought for {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}} in the {{ill|Genkō War|en|Genkō War|WD=Q1432624|lt=Genkō War|1=Genkō War}} to overthrow the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}} and restore power in Japan to the {{ill|Imperial Court in Kyoto|en|Imperial Court in Kyoto|WD=Q11680900|lt=Imperial Court|1=Imperial Court in Kyoto}}. Kusunoki was a leading figure of the [[Kenmu Restoration]] in 1333, and remained loyal to the unpopular Emperor Go-Daigo after {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} began to reverse the restoration in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō wars|1=Nanboku-chō period}} three years later. Kusunoki attacked Takauji in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu|1=Settsu Province}} at the command of the Emperor, an act of obedience sure to result in defeat, and died at the {{ill|Battle of Minatogawa|en|Battle of Minatogawa|WD=Q114620|lt=Battle of Minatogawa|1=Battle of Minatogawa}} in 1336. Kusunoki became a popular {{ill|en:Legend|en|en:Legend|WD=Q44342|lt=en:Legend|1=Legend}} in Japan representing loyalty and {{ill|Virtue|en|Virtue|WD=Q157811|lt=Virtue|1=Virtue}}, and associated with the phrase "Would that I had seven lives to give for my country!" (七生報國; "''Shichishō Hōkoku!''"). Kusunoki was posthumously awarded the highest court Rank in Japan, [[Senior First Rank]] (''shō ichi-i)'', by the {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|WD=Q1322181|lt=Government of Meiji Japan|1=Government of Meiji Japan}} in 1880, over 500 years after his death. He was highly evaluated as "Japan's three loyal retainers" along with Fujifusa Madenokoji and {{ill|Taira no Shigemori|en|Taira no Shigemori|WD=Q1184985|lt=Taira no Shigemori|1=Taira no Shigemori}}. ==Early life== Kusunoki Masashige's origin has not been validated and it was merely six years between the start of his military campaign in 1331 and his demise in 1336. Kusunoki is believed to have been born in 1294 in {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}} as a "well-to-do member of the rural {{ill|Gentry|en|Gentry|WD=Q844586|lt=Gentry|1=Gentry}}" and claimed descent from {{ill|Tachibana no Moroe|en|Tachibana no Moroe|WD=Q3135863|lt=Tachibana Moroe|1=Tachibana no Moroe}}, "a great nobleman" of the eighth century. His birthplace has been linked to the village of Chihaya-Akasaka where a small monument called the "Nanko Tanjochi" can be found. Kusunoki was a "scholar and a devout {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism in Japan}}" with much of his early education taking place at {{ill|Kanshin-ji|en|Kanshin-ji|WD=Q1088470|lt=Kanshin-ji|1=Kanshin-ji}} Temple in Kawachinagano, in present-day southern {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}.{{rp|53}} Later in his life, Kusunoki would arrange for considerable renovations to the temple. While studying at Kanshin-ji, he would make regular trips to central Kawachinagano to study strategy under the tutelage of a man named Oe Tokichika. According to legend, Emperor Go-Daigo had a dream in which he was sheltering under a [[:en:Camphor tree|Camphor tree]] ("''kusunoki''" in {{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|WD=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}}), and that this dream led him to the surname of the warrior who would support him.{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai, A Military History |publisher=MacMillan Publishing Co., Inc. |year=1977 |ISBN=0026205408 |page=95}}{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=158–159}} ==Military career== A brilliant tactician and strategist, Kusunoki's cunning defense of two key Loyalist fortresses at [[:en:Chihayaakasaka, Osaka|Akasaka]], the {{ill|Siege of Akasaka|en|Siege of Akasaka|WD=Q7509791|lt=Siege of Akasaka|1=Siege of Akasaka}}, and [[:en:Chihayaakasaka, Osaka|Chihaya]], the {{ill|Siege of Chihaya|en|Siege of Chihaya|WD=Q7509930|lt=Siege of Chihaya|1=Siege of Chihaya}}, helped allow Go-Daigo to briefly return to power.{{rp|160,164,173,175,180}} He lived during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}. In 1333, Go-Daigo rewarded Masashige with governorship of {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province|1=Izumi Province}} and {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}}. Furthermore, he was promoted to Fifth Rank. Later he was appointed to the Records Office and Settlements Board.{{Cite book |last=Morris |first=Ivan |title=The Nobility of Failure |publisher=Holt, Rinehart and Winston |year=1975 |ISBN=9780030108112 |pages=[https://archive.org/details/nobilityoffailur00morr/page/127 127] |url=https://archive.org/details/nobilityoffailur00morr/page/127 }} However, one of the loyalist generals, {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, betrayed Go-Daigo and led an army against Kusunoki and the remaining loyalists.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334–1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |pages=43–44}} Takauji was able to take Kyoto, but only temporarily before [[Nitta Yoshisada]] and Masashige were able to dislodge Takauji, forcing him to flee to the west. By 1336 however, Takauji was a threat to Kyoto again.{{rp|130}} Kusunoki suggested to the Emperor that they take refuge on sacred {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|WD=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}} and allow Takauji to take {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, only to swoop down from the mountain, and with the help of the monks of Mount Hiei, trap Takauji in the city and destroy him.{{rp|181–182}} Go-Daigo was unwilling to leave the capital however, and insisted that Kusunoki meet Takauji's superior forces in the field in a pitched battle. Kusunoki, in what would later be viewed as the ultimate act of samurai loyalty, obediently accepted his Emperor's foolish command and knowingly marched his army into almost certain death.{{rp|102-102}}{{rp|126}} The battle, which took place at {{ill|Battle of Minatogawa|en|Battle of Minatogawa|WD=Q114620|lt=Minatogawa|1=Battle of Minatogawa}} in modern-day {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kobe|en|Chūō-ku, Kobe|WD=Q686802|lt=Chūō-ku|1=Chūō-ku, Kobe}}, [[:en:Kobe, Hyōgo|Kobe]], was a tactical disaster. There are two accounts of the proposal made by Kusunoki Masashige to the emperor Go-Daigo, the [[Taiheiki]] and the ''Baisho Ron''. One was that they regroup and attack from two sides, the other was that they bring back general Takauji to their side thus balancing the scales. Both arguments were ignored.{{rp|181–183}}{{rp|50–52}} Kusunoki, his army completely surrounded, was down to only 50 of the original 700 horsemen. According to legend, his brother {{ill|Kusunoki Masasue|en|Kusunoki Masasue|WD=Q11541197|lt=Masasue|1=Kusunoki Masasue}}'s last words were ''Shichishō Hōkoku!'' (七生報國; "Would that I had seven lives to give for my country!") and Kusunoki Masashige agreed.{{rp|185–187}}{{rp|133}} Upon his death, his head was removed and sent to {{ill|Kanshin-ji|en|Kanshin-ji|WD=Q1088470|lt=Kanshin-ji|1=Kanshin-ji}} where it was buried in a {{ill|Kubizuka|en|Kubizuka|WD=Q11667022|lt=Kubizuka|1=Kubizuka}}. [[File:Kusunoki Kanshinji.jpg|thumb|Equestrian statue of Kusunoki Masashige at the entrance to {{ill|Kanshin-ji|en|Kanshin-ji|WD=Q1088470|lt=Kanshin-ji|1=Kanshin-ji}} in {{ill|Kawachinagano|en|Kawachinagano|WD=Q819572|lt=Kawachinagano|1=Kawachinagano}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}.]] He is also thought to have built a number of smaller castles throughout southern Osaka, particularly within what is now the city of {{ill|Kawachinagano|en|Kawachinagano|WD=Q819572|lt=Kawachinagano|1=Kawachinagano}}. Eboshigata Castle and Ishibotoke Castle were both built along the route of the Koya Kaido, a popular pilgrimage trail stretching between Kyoto and [[:en:Koyasan|Koyasan]]. These castles were designed not only to protect the trail from bandits but also as an important source of income and intelligence as travelers were obliged to pay a toll and the garrison would listen out for rumours and news from around Japan. ==Legacy== [[File:Tachi Sword - Kagemitsu.jpg|thumb| Kagemitsu Sword of Kusunoki Masashige, also called Little Dragon Kagemitsu (Koryū Kagemitsu) after a relief on the face of the blade, curvature: 2.7 cm (1.1 in) Kamakura period, May 1322 80.6 cm (31.7 in) Tokyo National Museum, Tokyo]] His son, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|Kusunoki Masatsura|WD=Q966298|lt=Kusunoki Masatsura|1=Kusunoki Masatsura}}, served the emperor's successor, the 12-year-old [[:en:Go-Murakami|Go-Murakami]], in a relationship of reciprocal trust and devotion mirroring the figure of his father Kusunoki and keeping the flame of loyalist resistance alive. Masatsura died alongside his brother Masatoki and cousin Wada Takahide in a battle that saw the end of the Kusunoki clan and there followed a less-than-ideal scramble for power and gain among the Courts.{{rp|103}} Kusunoki "stands in the history of his country as the ideal figure of a warrior, compact of civil and military virtues in a high degree."{{rp|53}} The parting of Masashige with his son "used to be included in all elementary school readers and was the subject of a patriotic song which was popular in Japanese schools before {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}."{{rp|131}} Masashige had a [[tachi]] called Little Dragon Kagemitsu (小龍景光, Koryū Kagemitsu). An elaborate Kurikara dragon was carved on the handle. Originally, the dragon's appearance was visible on the blade, but later, in the process of cutting off the handle and shortening the length, the dragon's body was hidden by the handle. The dragon is a manifestation of [[Acala]]. ==Legend== After the full-scale introduction of {{ill|Neo-Confucianism|en|Neo-Confucianism|WD=Q825754|lt=Neo-Confucianism|1=Neo-Confucianism}} as a state philosophy by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], Kusunoki Masashige, once called a traitor by the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court|1=Northern Court}}, was resurrected with {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}} as a precursor of Sinocentric absolutists, based upon the Neo-Confucian theories.{{Citation needed|date=November 2021}} During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, scholars and samurai who were influenced by the Neo-Confucian theories popularized the legend of Kusunoki and enshrined him as a patriotic hero, called '''Nankō''' (楠公) or '''Dai Nankō''' (大楠公), who epitomized {{ill|Loyalty|en|Loyalty|WD=Q1132131|lt=Loyalty|1=Loyalty}}, courage, and {{ill|Faith|en|Faith|WD=Q5410500|lt=devotion|1=Faith}} to the Emperor.{{Citation needed|date=November 2021}} In 1871 [[Minatogawa Shrine]] is established in order to enshrine the [[kami]] spirit of Kusunoki Masashige.{{Citation needed|date=November 2021}} Kusunoki later became a patron saint of sorts to {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} {{ill|Kamikaze|en|Kamikaze|WD=Q133194|lt=Kamikaze|1=Kamikaze}}, who saw themselves as his spiritual heirs in sacrificing their lives for the Emperor.{{Citation needed|date=November 2021}} == Family == Brother: * {{ill|Kusunoki Masasue|en|Kusunoki Masasue|WD=Q11541197|lt=Kusunoki Masasue|1=Kusunoki Masasue}} Children: * {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|Kusunoki Masatsura|WD=Q966298|lt=Kusunoki Masatsura|1=Kusunoki Masatsura}} * {{ill|Kusunoki Masanori|en|Kusunoki Masanori|WD=Q11541193|lt=Kusunoki Masanori|1=Kusunoki Masanori}} * {{ill|Kusunoki Masatoki|en|Kusunoki Masatoki|WD=Q11541199|lt=Kusunoki Masatoki|1=Kusunoki Masatoki}} ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (July 20, 1880; posthumous) ==See also== * {{ill|Nathan Hale|en|Nathan Hale|WD=Q465574|lt=Nathan Hale|1=Nathan Hale}}— {{ill|Americans|en|Americans|WD=Q846570|lt=American|1=Americans}} {{ill|Patriot (American Revolution)|en|Patriot (American Revolution)|WD=Q1365374|lt=Patriot|1=Patriot (American Revolution)}}, soldier, and spy for the {{ill|Continental Army|en|Continental Army|WD=Q54122|lt=Continental Army|1=Continental Army}} during the {{ill|American Revolutionary War|en|American Revolutionary War|WD=Q40949|lt=American Revolutionary War|1=American Revolutionary War}}. He volunteered for an intelligence-gathering mission in {{ill|New York City|en|New York City|WD=Q60|lt=New York City|1=New York City}} but was captured by the {{ill|British people|en|British people|WD=Q842438|lt=British|1=British people}} and executed by {{ill|hanging|en|hanging|WD=Q175111|lt=hanging|1=Hanging}}. His last words were, reportedly, "I only regret that I have but one life to lose for my country." If he was intending to paraphrase or quote anyone, however, it was likely {{ill|Joseph Addison|en|Joseph Addison|WD=Q206384|lt=Joseph Addison|1=Joseph Addison}}'s play {{ill|Cato, a Tragedy|en|Cato, a Tragedy|WD=Q5053587|lt=Cato, a Tragedy|1=Cato, a Tragedy}} and not Kusunoki Masashige. * {{ill|Otoya Yamaguchi|en|Otoya Yamaguchi|WD=Q469406|lt=Otoya Yamaguchi|1=Otoya Yamaguchi}}— 17-year-old Japanese right-wing {{ill|Ultranationalism|en|Ultranationalism|WD=Q38352698|lt=ultranationalist|1=Ultranationalism}} who assassinated socialist politician [[:en:Inejiro Asanuma|Inejiro Asanuma]] during a televised debate in 1960. After being arrested and interrogated, Yamaguchi committed suicide in a detention facility less than three weeks after the assassination. Before committing suicide, Yamaguchi wrote with toothpaste on his cell wall, "Long live the {{ill|Emperor|en|Emperor|WD=Q39018|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor}}" (天皇陛下万才, ''tennōheika banzai'') and "Would that I had seven lives to give for my country" (七生報国, ''shichishō hōkoku'')). ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Kusunoki Masashige}} [[en:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[ca:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[cs:Masašige Kusunoki]] [[da:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[de:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[eo:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[es:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[fa:کوسونوکی ماساشیگه]] [[fi:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[fr:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[id:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[it:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[ja:楠木正成]] [[ka:კუსუნოკი მასასიგე]] [[ko:구스노키 마사시게]] [[nl:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[pl:Masashige Kusunoki]] [[pt:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[ru:Кусуноки Масасигэ]] [[simple:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[sq:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[sr:Кусуноки Масашиге]] [[th:คูซูโนกิ มาซาชิเงะ]] [[tr:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[uk:Кусунокі Масашіґе]] [[wuu:楠木正成]] [[zh:楠木正成]] {{wikidata link|Q382234}} [[Category:1336 deaths]] [[Category:Deified Japanese men]] [[Category:Genkō War]] [[Category:People of Kamakura-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Nanboku-chō-period Japan]] [[Category:Samurai]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

samurai}} {{Expand Japanese|topic=bio|楠木正成|date=October 2021}} {{family name hatnote|Kusunoki|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Kusunoki Masashige | honorific_suffix = [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] | native_name = 楠木正成 | native_name_lang = ja | image = File:Kusunoki Masashige.jpg | caption = Portrait of Kusunoki Masashige by {{ill|Kanō Sanraku|en|Kanō Sanraku|qid=Q3064625|lt=Kanō Sanraku|1=Kanō Sanraku}}, {{circa|before 1635}} | birth_date = 1294 | birth_place ={{ill|Kawachi Province|qid=Q1047150|1=Kawachi Province}} | death_date = {{death date|1336|7|4}} | death_place ={{ill|Minato River|qid=Q11563141|1=Minato River}} | resting_place = {{ill|Kanshin-ji|qid=Q1088470|1=Kanshin-ji}} | other_names = Dai Nankō, Hyōe-no-Jō, Saemon-no-Jō, Jō | occupation = {{ill|bushi|qid=Q1975935|1=bushi}}; {{ill|samurai|qid=Q38142|1=Samurai}} | years_active = | known_for = Overthrowing the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|qid=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, ideal samurai loyalty | notable_works = | monuments = Hōken-tō
Various statues | gender= {{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} | nationality= {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17|1=Japan}} | title= {{ill|Kebiishi|qid=Q497217|1=Office of Police and Justice}}; {{ill|Saemonnojō|qid=Q11479716|1=Saemonnojō}}; {{ill|bugyō|qid=Q2535820|1=Bugyō}}; {{ill|shugo|qid=Q1141023|1=Shugo}} | children= {{ill|Kusunoki Masanori|qid=Q11541193|1=Kusunoki Masanori}}; {{ill|Kusunoki Masatoki|qid=Q11541199|1=Kusunoki Masatoki}}; {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|qid=Q966298|1=Kusunoki Masatsura}} | father= {{ill|Kusunoki Masatoyo|qid=Q11541204|1=Kusunoki Masatoyo}} | relatives= {{ill|Kusunoki Masasue|qid=Q11541197|1=Kusunoki Masasue}} | family= {{ill|Kusunoki clan|qid=Q11541208|1=Kusunoki clan}} | organization= {{ill|Q11507117|qid=Q11507117|1=Q11507117}}; {{ill|Sanboku Issō|qid=Q11355573|1=Sanboku Issō}} | given_name= {{ill|Masashige|qid=Q6782681|1=Masashige}} }} [[Image:Kusunoki Masashige.JPG|right|thumb|300px|{{ill|Equestrian statue|en|Equestrian statue|qid=Q659396|lt=Equestrian statue|1=Equestrian statue}} of Kusunoki Masashige outside the [[:en:Imperial Palace of Japan|Imperial Palace]] in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}.]] [[Image:Kusunoki Masashige statue.jpg|right|thumb|The same statue from a different angle, close-up.]] {{Nihongo|'''Kusunoki Masashige'''|楠木 正成||1294 – 4 July 1336}} was a Japanese military commander and {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} of the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} remembered as the ideal loyal samurai. Kusunoki fought for {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|qid=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}} in the {{ill|Genkō War|en|Genkō War|qid=Q1432624|lt=Genkō War|1=Genkō War}} to overthrow the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|qid=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}} and restore power in Japan to the {{ill|Imperial Court in Kyoto|en|Imperial Court in Kyoto|qid=Q11680900|lt=Imperial Court|1=Imperial Court in Kyoto}}. Kusunoki was a leading figure of the [[Kenmu Restoration]] in 1333, and remained loyal to the unpopular Emperor Go-Daigo after {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|qid=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} began to reverse the restoration in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|qid=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō wars|1=Nanboku-chō period}} three years later. Kusunoki attacked Takauji in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|qid=Q864163|lt=Settsu|1=Settsu Province}} at the command of the Emperor, an act of obedience sure to result in defeat, and died at the {{ill|Battle of Minatogawa|en|Battle of Minatogawa|qid=Q114620|lt=Battle of Minatogawa|1=Battle of Minatogawa}} in 1336. Kusunoki became a popular {{ill|en:Legend|en|en:Legend|qid=Q44342|lt=en:Legend|1=Legend}} in Japan representing loyalty and {{ill|Virtue|en|Virtue|qid=Q157811|lt=Virtue|1=Virtue}}, and associated with the phrase "Would that I had seven lives to give for my country!" (七生報國; "''Shichishō Hōkoku!''"). Kusunoki was posthumously awarded the highest court Rank in Japan, [[Senior First Rank]] (''shō ichi-i)'', by the {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|qid=Q1322181|lt=Government of Meiji Japan|1=Government of Meiji Japan}} in 1880, over 500 years after his death. He was highly evaluated as "Japan's three loyal retainers" along with Fujifusa Madenokoji and {{ill|Taira no Shigemori|en|Taira no Shigemori|qid=Q1184985|lt=Taira no Shigemori|1=Taira no Shigemori}}. ==Early life== Kusunoki Masashige's origin has not been validated and it was merely six years between the start of his military campaign in 1331 and his demise in 1336. Kusunoki is believed to have been born in 1294 in {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|qid=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}} as a "well-to-do member of the rural {{ill|Gentry|en|Gentry|qid=Q844586|lt=Gentry|1=Gentry}}" and claimed descent from {{ill|Tachibana no Moroe|en|Tachibana no Moroe|qid=Q3135863|lt=Tachibana Moroe|1=Tachibana no Moroe}}, "a great nobleman" of the eighth century. His birthplace has been linked to the village of Chihaya-Akasaka where a small monument called the "Nanko Tanjochi" can be found. Kusunoki was a "scholar and a devout {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism in Japan}}" with much of his early education taking place at {{ill|Kanshin-ji|en|Kanshin-ji|qid=Q1088470|lt=Kanshin-ji|1=Kanshin-ji}} Temple in Kawachinagano, in present-day southern {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}.{{rp|53}} Later in his life, Kusunoki would arrange for considerable renovations to the temple. While studying at Kanshin-ji, he would make regular trips to central Kawachinagano to study strategy under the tutelage of a man named Oe Tokichika. According to legend, Emperor Go-Daigo had a dream in which he was sheltering under a [[:en:Camphor tree|Camphor tree]] ("''kusunoki''" in {{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|qid=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}}), and that this dream led him to the surname of the warrior who would support him.{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai, A Military History |publisher=MacMillan Publishing Co., Inc. |year=1977 |ISBN=0026205408 |page=95}}{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=158–159}} ==Military career== A brilliant tactician and strategist, Kusunoki's cunning defense of two key Loyalist fortresses at [[:en:Chihayaakasaka, Osaka|Akasaka]], the {{ill|Siege of Akasaka|en|Siege of Akasaka|qid=Q7509791|lt=Siege of Akasaka|1=Siege of Akasaka}}, and [[:en:Chihayaakasaka, Osaka|Chihaya]], the {{ill|Siege of Chihaya|en|Siege of Chihaya|qid=Q7509930|lt=Siege of Chihaya|1=Siege of Chihaya}}, helped allow Go-Daigo to briefly return to power.{{rp|160,164,173,175,180}} He lived during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}. In 1333, Go-Daigo rewarded Masashige with governorship of {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|qid=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province|1=Izumi Province}} and {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|qid=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}}. Furthermore, he was promoted to Fifth Rank. Later he was appointed to the Records Office and Settlements Board.{{Cite book |last=Morris |first=Ivan |title=The Nobility of Failure |publisher=Holt, Rinehart and Winston |year=1975 |ISBN=9780030108112 |pages=[https://archive.org/details/nobilityoffailur00morr/page/127 127] |url=https://archive.org/details/nobilityoffailur00morr/page/127 }} However, one of the loyalist generals, {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|qid=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, betrayed Go-Daigo and led an army against Kusunoki and the remaining loyalists.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334–1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |pages=43–44}} Takauji was able to take Kyoto, but only temporarily before [[Nitta Yoshisada]] and Masashige were able to dislodge Takauji, forcing him to flee to the west. By 1336 however, Takauji was a threat to Kyoto again.{{rp|130}} Kusunoki suggested to the Emperor that they take refuge on sacred {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|qid=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}} and allow Takauji to take {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, only to swoop down from the mountain, and with the help of the monks of Mount Hiei, trap Takauji in the city and destroy him.{{rp|181–182}} Go-Daigo was unwilling to leave the capital however, and insisted that Kusunoki meet Takauji's superior forces in the field in a pitched battle. Kusunoki, in what would later be viewed as the ultimate act of samurai loyalty, obediently accepted his Emperor's foolish command and knowingly marched his army into almost certain death.{{rp|102-102}}{{rp|126}} The battle, which took place at {{ill|Battle of Minatogawa|en|Battle of Minatogawa|qid=Q114620|lt=Minatogawa|1=Battle of Minatogawa}} in modern-day {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kobe|en|Chūō-ku, Kobe|qid=Q686802|lt=Chūō-ku|1=Chūō-ku, Kobe}}, [[:en:Kobe, Hyōgo|Kobe]], was a tactical disaster. There are two accounts of the proposal made by Kusunoki Masashige to the emperor Go-Daigo, the [[Taiheiki]] and the ''Baisho Ron''. One was that they regroup and attack from two sides, the other was that they bring back general Takauji to their side thus balancing the scales. Both arguments were ignored.{{rp|181–183}}{{rp|50–52}} Kusunoki, his army completely surrounded, was down to only 50 of the original 700 horsemen. According to legend, his brother {{ill|Kusunoki Masasue|en|Kusunoki Masasue|qid=Q11541197|lt=Masasue|1=Kusunoki Masasue}}'s last words were ''Shichishō Hōkoku!'' (七生報國; "Would that I had seven lives to give for my country!") and Kusunoki Masashige agreed.{{rp|185–187}}{{rp|133}} Upon his death, his head was removed and sent to {{ill|Kanshin-ji|en|Kanshin-ji|qid=Q1088470|lt=Kanshin-ji|1=Kanshin-ji}} where it was buried in a {{ill|Kubizuka|en|Kubizuka|qid=Q11667022|lt=Kubizuka|1=Kubizuka}}. [[File:Kusunoki Kanshinji.jpg|thumb|Equestrian statue of Kusunoki Masashige at the entrance to {{ill|Kanshin-ji|en|Kanshin-ji|qid=Q1088470|lt=Kanshin-ji|1=Kanshin-ji}} in {{ill|Kawachinagano|en|Kawachinagano|qid=Q819572|lt=Kawachinagano|1=Kawachinagano}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}.]] He is also thought to have built a number of smaller castles throughout southern Osaka, particularly within what is now the city of {{ill|Kawachinagano|en|Kawachinagano|qid=Q819572|lt=Kawachinagano|1=Kawachinagano}}. Eboshigata Castle and Ishibotoke Castle were both built along the route of the Koya Kaido, a popular pilgrimage trail stretching between Kyoto and [[:en:Koyasan|Koyasan]]. These castles were designed not only to protect the trail from bandits but also as an important source of income and intelligence as travelers were obliged to pay a toll and the garrison would listen out for rumours and news from around Japan. ==Legacy== [[File:Tachi Sword - Kagemitsu.jpg|thumb| Kagemitsu Sword of Kusunoki Masashige, also called Little Dragon Kagemitsu (Koryū Kagemitsu) after a relief on the face of the blade, curvature: 2.7 cm (1.1 in) Kamakura period, May 1322 80.6 cm (31.7 in) Tokyo National Museum, Tokyo]] His son, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|Kusunoki Masatsura|qid=Q966298|lt=Kusunoki Masatsura|1=Kusunoki Masatsura}}, served the emperor's successor, the 12-year-old [[:en:Go-Murakami|Go-Murakami]], in a relationship of reciprocal trust and devotion mirroring the figure of his father Kusunoki and keeping the flame of loyalist resistance alive. Masatsura died alongside his brother Masatoki and cousin Wada Takahide in a battle that saw the end of the Kusunoki clan and there followed a less-than-ideal scramble for power and gain among the Courts.{{rp|103}} Kusunoki "stands in the history of his country as the ideal figure of a warrior, compact of civil and military virtues in a high degree."{{rp|53}} The parting of Masashige with his son "used to be included in all elementary school readers and was the subject of a patriotic song which was popular in Japanese schools before {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}."{{rp|131}} Masashige had a [[tachi]] called Little Dragon Kagemitsu (小龍景光, Koryū Kagemitsu). An elaborate Kurikara dragon was carved on the handle. Originally, the dragon's appearance was visible on the blade, but later, in the process of cutting off the handle and shortening the length, the dragon's body was hidden by the handle. The dragon is a manifestation of [[Acala]]. ==Legend== After the full-scale introduction of {{ill|Neo-Confucianism|en|Neo-Confucianism|qid=Q825754|lt=Neo-Confucianism|1=Neo-Confucianism}} as a state philosophy by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], Kusunoki Masashige, once called a traitor by the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|qid=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court|1=Northern Court}}, was resurrected with {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|qid=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}} as a precursor of Sinocentric absolutists, based upon the Neo-Confucian theories.{{Citation needed|date=November 2021}} During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, scholars and samurai who were influenced by the Neo-Confucian theories popularized the legend of Kusunoki and enshrined him as a patriotic hero, called '''Nankō''' (楠公) or '''Dai Nankō''' (大楠公), who epitomized {{ill|Loyalty|en|Loyalty|qid=Q1132131|lt=Loyalty|1=Loyalty}}, courage, and {{ill|Faith|en|Faith|qid=Q5410500|lt=devotion|1=Faith}} to the Emperor.{{Citation needed|date=November 2021}} In 1871 [[Minatogawa Shrine]] is established in order to enshrine the [[kami]] spirit of Kusunoki Masashige.{{Citation needed|date=November 2021}} Kusunoki later became a patron saint of sorts to {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} {{ill|Kamikaze|en|Kamikaze|qid=Q133194|lt=Kamikaze|1=Kamikaze}}, who saw themselves as his spiritual heirs in sacrificing their lives for the Emperor.{{Citation needed|date=November 2021}} == Family == Brother: * {{ill|Kusunoki Masasue|en|Kusunoki Masasue|qid=Q11541197|lt=Kusunoki Masasue|1=Kusunoki Masasue}} Children: * {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|Kusunoki Masatsura|qid=Q966298|lt=Kusunoki Masatsura|1=Kusunoki Masatsura}} * {{ill|Kusunoki Masanori|en|Kusunoki Masanori|qid=Q11541193|lt=Kusunoki Masanori|1=Kusunoki Masanori}} * {{ill|Kusunoki Masatoki|en|Kusunoki Masatoki|qid=Q11541199|lt=Kusunoki Masatoki|1=Kusunoki Masatoki}} ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (July 20, 1880; posthumous) ==See also== * {{ill|Nathan Hale|en|Nathan Hale|qid=Q465574|lt=Nathan Hale|1=Nathan Hale}}— {{ill|Americans|en|Americans|qid=Q846570|lt=American|1=Americans}} {{ill|Patriot (American Revolution)|en|Patriot (American Revolution)|qid=Q1365374|lt=Patriot|1=Patriot (American Revolution)}}, soldier, and spy for the {{ill|Continental Army|en|Continental Army|qid=Q54122|lt=Continental Army|1=Continental Army}} during the {{ill|American Revolutionary War|en|American Revolutionary War|qid=Q40949|lt=American Revolutionary War|1=American Revolutionary War}}. He volunteered for an intelligence-gathering mission in {{ill|New York City|en|New York City|qid=Q60|lt=New York City|1=New York City}} but was captured by the {{ill|British people|en|British people|qid=Q842438|lt=British|1=British people}} and executed by {{ill|hanging|en|hanging|qid=Q175111|lt=hanging|1=Hanging}}. His last words were, reportedly, "I only regret that I have but one life to lose for my country." If he was intending to paraphrase or quote anyone, however, it was likely {{ill|Joseph Addison|en|Joseph Addison|qid=Q206384|lt=Joseph Addison|1=Joseph Addison}}'s play {{ill|Cato, a Tragedy|en|Cato, a Tragedy|qid=Q5053587|lt=Cato, a Tragedy|1=Cato, a Tragedy}} and not Kusunoki Masashige. * {{ill|Otoya Yamaguchi|en|Otoya Yamaguchi|qid=Q469406|lt=Otoya Yamaguchi|1=Otoya Yamaguchi}}— 17-year-old Japanese right-wing {{ill|Ultranationalism|en|Ultranationalism|qid=Q38352698|lt=ultranationalist|1=Ultranationalism}} who assassinated socialist politician [[:en:Inejiro Asanuma|Inejiro Asanuma]] during a televised debate in 1960. After being arrested and interrogated, Yamaguchi committed suicide in a detention facility less than three weeks after the assassination. Before committing suicide, Yamaguchi wrote with toothpaste on his cell wall, "Long live the {{ill|Emperor|en|Emperor|qid=Q39018|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor}}" (天皇陛下万才, ''tennōheika banzai'') and "Would that I had seven lives to give for my country" (七生報国, ''shichishō hōkoku'')). ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Kusunoki Masashige}} [[en:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[ca:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[cs:Masašige Kusunoki]] [[da:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[de:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[eo:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[es:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[fa:کوسونوکی ماساشیگه]] [[fi:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[fr:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[id:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[it:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[ja:楠木正成]] [[ka:კუსუნოკი მასასიგე]] [[ko:구스노키 마사시게]] [[nl:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[pl:Masashige Kusunoki]] [[pt:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[ru:Кусуноки Масасигэ]] [[simple:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[sq:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[sr:Кусуноки Масашиге]] [[th:คูซูโนกิ มาซาชิเงะ]] [[tr:Kusunoki Masashige]] [[uk:Кусунокі Масашіґе]] [[wuu:楠木正成]] [[zh:楠木正成]] {{wikidata link|Q382234}} [[Category:1336 deaths]] [[Category:Deified Japanese men]] [[Category:Genkō War]] [[Category:People of Kamakura-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Nanboku-chō-period Japan]] [[Category:Samurai]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
